title: Why does the electron have charge -e?

content:
A categorization for debates #In recent times, both personally and in the public discourse, there has been a significant focus on debates regarding current events. Oftentimes, it seems like two parties are talking past each other and one has to wonder, “what is the point of this debate?”
Actually, this is a good question, what is the point of a debate? I think a possible categorization can be made by considering who the outcome of the debate will affect. For this, I think the following three categories will be sufficient:
Frivolous debates: Debates whose outcomes will not meaningfully affect anyone. Personal debates: Debates whose outcomes will only affect the parties debating. Societal debates: Debates whose outcomes will affect more than just the parties debating. Maybe a better question I want to answer is the following: Using this categorization, when is it productive to have certain debates?
What do I mean by productive? I mean a debate that will change the actions or affect at least one party or others.
Frivolous debates #Frivolous debates are those that will not meaningfully affect anyone. These can be debates of opinion such as “I think this movie is better than this one” or “I think <> is a better artist than <>”. Whether these debates are subjective or not is besides the point, the result of the debate will not change how either party behaves. These debates are not productive, but can be fun to have.
Matters of opinion are not the only things that belong in this category. Debates that one would expect to be in the other two categories (Personal or Societal) can land in the Frivolous category in certain cases. This occurs when at least one of the parties are just talking past the other and have completely made up their mind beforehand. No matter what party A says or evidence it produces, party B will not acknowledge A’s points because B did not come in with the possibility of changing their mind. These debates are not productive, and are also infuriating to have.
While not an implication, these types of debates usually do not need concrete pieces of evidence (historical, scientific, statistical, etc.) and even if they are presented, they are often not the defining pieces argument.
In either case, no one will change their behavior from this type of debate and it is not a productive endeavor. I want to emphasize that subjectivity of the “right” answer in no way plays into this categorization.
Personal debates #Personal debates are those whose outcomes will affect the parties debating. Many things fall into this category. For instance, maybe a friend debates with me to convince me I need to get more exercise. This affects me personally, but not anyone else in a meaningful way.
I think most academic discussions fall into this category as well. Two parties want to solve or understand a common problem or concept, and the outcome will only affect the two parties. Maybe in changing how one or both parties choose to approach or explain the issue. Often, one can disagree on several points but concede, “in order to figure this out, we need to do this calculation” or “we need to understand this point”,
These debates are productive and worth having because they will affect someone; however, they can quickly slip into being Frivolous. If party A presents a sufficient amount of evidence that any reasonable person could see that party B is wrong, or even if party B concedes the point, party B still has no obligation to act in accordance with what the debate was about. Just because all the evidence points to me having to exercise to stay healthy, I have no obligation to do so. While the discussion is not a productive one, I am perfectly in my right to not act and this is fine. It’s just that, in this case, the debate is frivolous.
Personal debates can have a broad spectrum of evidence to back them up. While statistics and reputable articles are still the gold standard, in certain cases, anecdotal evidence is still appreciated. Maybe party A says the best BBQ is a certain location but party B has been to several other locations and convinces A to go somewhere else which B thinks is the best.
What may seem to be Personal or Frivolous debates can often slip into Societal debates. This happens for issues in which personal beliefs or tendencies are reflected in policies when that person votes or places an active role in a position that affects other people. The reason to be mindful about this transition is Societal debates require stronger evidence than Personal (and certainly Frivolous) debates.
If a CEO is convinced anecdotally that 5'6" people work the hardest, that is their right. But if they start selecting only 5'6" employees, the burden of proof is much higher. If you try and convince a friend that scientific investment is useless/necessary, that is a societal debate; they can go on to vote for either of those things.
Societal debates #Societal debates are perhaps the scariest as the outcomes affect others. Political debates would fall into a (perhaps large) subclass of Societal debates. Of course, as mentioned in the previous section, these debates do not just occur by those in power, they also occur in our everyday lives and affect our everyday lives, even if not apparent. After voting, maybe the current economy as a product of the government forces your company to produce layoffs, or maybe congress cuts the budget of a key program. In a functioning democracy, even inaction as an outcome is an action.
When debating things that could change people’s lives, the highest standard of evidence is required. This should be well respected, peer studied articles, statistics, or historical events. Anecdotes should not suffice to make decisions for other people as one’s personal experience can be skewed. A rather famous example is that in 2025, roughly half of Americans thought crime in America was worse than in 2024 (see this Gallup poll) while the FBI statistics show a 4.5% decrease.
If two parties cannot agree on the facts and statistics, a productive societal debate cannot occur. Partially, this is why I think live debates are usually not productive. These statistics are not things one should memorize, but cite as needed. In the heat of a debate, one can forget such facts or statistics or simply not know them. In bad faith live debates, one can cite a skewed or made up statistic and both parties just have to take it as given.
It is often the case that these debates occur not to convince the parties debating, but to convince spectators. This too seems to be poor practice. Just because one forgets evidence or is just a bad debater, does not mean their point is wrong. Just because one is charismatic in a debate does not mean they are right. In an ideal world, the spectators would take just the points of the parties, not their appearance, nor their demeanor, and fact check them or critically think about these points.
Academics, (ex. Political and Economic theorists) rarely argue their points in front of a live audience. There is too much to keep track of and it is not a productive when topics are nuanced and take time to digest. Instead, they spread ideas and respond to each other with papers or in depth discussions that take time. Indeed, time is probably one of the biggest factors in having a productive Societal debate.
When the federalists were debating the ratification of the constitution in the late 1780’s, their primary tool was the Federalist Papers, a series of articles and essays in favor of the constitution. These were published in newspapers and gave both opponents and supporters time to digest and respond to the papers.
In some form, I guess I am advocating that Societal debates should be relegated to pen and paper or in long terms discussions where both parties can discuss the evidence and validity of the evidence for their points.
What do we gain? #What do we gain from the above construction? From the categorization, and the classification of evidence? Well, before committing to a debate, it is productive to consider which type of debate you are getting into. A priori you know what type of evidence you need to produce and how seriously you need to take the debate.
If it is a Frivolous debate, (even if pretending to be one of the others), it is not worth taking it seriously. You know it will not be a productive conversation as no one will change their mind. If it is a Societal debate, you know to have a productive conversation, you will need time and reputable evidence.
It is an excellent idea to clarify with who you are debating with what kind of debate you are having. Maybe not in terms of the above classification, but just ask, “Why are we debating this point?”
To conclude, before you get into an argument, consider its purpose, whether it can be productive, and what evidence you need to justify you point.
summary: A categorization for debates #In recent times, both personally and in the public discourse, there has been a significant focus on debates regarding current events.
title: What is the point of a debate?

content:
This is a companion piece to my friend’s post where they talk more about the coding side of this project. I’ve been learning algebraic topology recently for a variety of physics reasons and came upon this theorem.
The project can be found here: https://borsuk-ulam-tp.streamlit.app/
Colombia and Borneo sometimes have the same temperature #Recently, for a variety of reasons I’ll get into later, I’ve gotten interested in learning algebraic topology. While going through this series of lectures, I found one interesting result to be the Borsuk-Ulam theorem. It says for a continuous map $$f:S^n \to \mathbb{R}^n , ~\exists x \in S^n \text{ s.t. }f(x)=f(-x).$$ What does this mean in laymen’s terms?
\(f\) is a function that eats coordinates on a sphere (say longitude and latitude on Earth) and spits out two numbers (say temperature and pressure). Essentially, if we let \((\theta,\phi) \) be coordinates on Earth, $$f(\theta,\phi) = (T,P)~,$$ then we are guaranteed by Borsuk-Ulam that there are at least two points on opposite sides of the planet have the same temperature and pressure. The title is an allusion to the fact that (at the time of this post) there seems to be a matching between east Colombia and west Borneo that happens relatively often.
Note that temperature and pressure aren’t special here, it’s just the mandatory example that must be covered every time Borsuk-Ulam is brought up in a class.
Crucially, the proof of this theorem isn’t constructive, it just asserts that there exists a point \(x\) on the sphere that does this.
After the lecture, I thought to myself, “well, it seems like you could code something up to actually find these points for you,” and I reached out to my friend to help me. We came up with a program that looks for these points (though honestly I was more there for emotional support). The project turned out even better than I imagined, starting its life as a jupyter notebook and then ending up as a program with an entire dashboard.
You can learn more about the code and some troubles we faced on his blog. For now, I promised to cover some math.
A (sketch of the) proof for the circle #I’ll try to sketch a proof with as little complicated math as possible. At the very least, one can understand the baby example (\(f:S^1 \to \mathbb{R}\) ) with just some knowledge of calculus 1.
Some notation here. \(S^1\) is just the regular old circle. So we are looking for (continuous) functions (basically functions that you can draw without taking your pen off the page) that take in a coordinate of the circle \(\theta\) and spit out a number \(f(\theta)\). An example would be \(f(\theta) = \sin \theta\) .
Let’s take \(x\) to be a point on the circle and \(-x\) to be a point on the opposite side. In practice, we would take \(x=\theta\) and \(-x = \theta+\pi\) (180 degrees around the circle).
We want to show that there exists a point \(x\) such that \( f(x) = f(-x)\). Let us define $$g(x) := f(x) - f(-x).$$ Here is the key insight: note that when \(f(x) = f(-x)\), \(g(x) = 0\). Also note that $$g(-x) = f(-x) - f(x) = -g(x).$$
Let’s say you pick a random point on the circle and find \(g(x)>0\).
The point \(x\) you chose does not satisfy \(f(x) = f(-x)\).
But on the opposite side of the circle, we know \(g(-x) = -g(x) < 0\).
So our function \(g\) went from positive on the circle to negative on the circle, which means that at some point, it must have crossed 0 (intermediate value theorem) somewhere along the circle. \(\Box\)
In the gif above, I picked a random function that has sines and cosines. a point and its antipode are plotted. The green line is the function \(g(x)\) and when it hits zero, we can see that the circle lights up green.
A (not even sketch of the) proof for the sphere #The sphere case is analogous, though a bit harder. There are a number of ways to prove it. Instead of covering it, I’ll just point to 3blue1brown’s video which does a better job than I ever could and also has helpful visuals. It also has the benefit of showing how it could be useful in the real world.
Because my motivation is algebraic topology, I’ll give the punchline for the algebraic topology proof. The so called fundamental group \(\pi_1(S^1) = \mathbb{Z}\) measures how many ways there are to wrap a circle around a circle and come back to the point you started at. It is isomorphic to the integers.
For instance, \( [0] \in \mathbb{Z}\) means no wrapping around the circle. \( [1] \in \mathbb{Z}\) means to wrap around once counterclockwise and \([-1]\) clockwise. We can add these just like the integers, \([1] + [1] = [2]\), or just that wrapping around once 2 times is equivalent to wrapping around twice once. In other words: $$ 1+1=2.$$ If we assume that Borsuk-Ulam does not hold, it can be shown that you can only wrap around the circle an odd number of times, not an even number of times. This is obviously false, why would I only be able to go around the circle 1,3, or 5 times and not twice? Thus, Borsuk-Ulam must hold \(\Box\).
summary: This is a companion piece to my friend’s post where they talk more about the coding side of this project.
title: Colombia and Borneo sometimes have the same temperature+pressure

content:
End of classes #I have just crowned the list of classes I have taken in my academic career with supersymmetry and I won’t be taking any other class. It feels weird to come to the end of this journey, especially when one has been taking college classes for the better part of 5 years, and in school for more than two decades.
A physics degree consists of playing catch up to modern day physics. We start by learning classical mechanics (1700s) then moving on to electromagnetism (mid 1800s) and then quantum mechanics and statistical mechanics (early 1900s). Quantum field theory takes us to the 1950s and on to around the 1970s. If one takes a string theory class, they learn about topics from the 1980’s onward (maybe to 1997 with AdS/CFT).
Thus, it is a nice ending point for classes (for which the community should have widespread agreement on material) when the supersymmetry class ends with the duality cascade (2004). So with this class, we are finally taken into the current millennium.
On the bright side, I will no longer have to worry about exams, homework, or grades. At the end of the day, the classes were starting to get in the way of research, and anything I need to learn I feel comfortable teaching myself at this point.
While I don’t plan to take any courses for credit (or any current physics classes), I do still plan on sitting in on interesting math classes relevant to my research. Luckily, UT makes this very easy to do.
A retrospective on classes #In these 5 years, I’ve taken a lot of classes, Some of them were very useful, some of them were not (most of the computer science classes). I thought it may be fun to go through and make a table.
Is this a flex that I have 2 degrees? Yes, because that is the only use for my CS degree now.
This is roughly in chronological order. When the class name is not enough, there is a description. Classes that are 2 semesters are only listed once and bolded (ex E&M 1 and 2 are listed as just E&M).
Undergraduate
✓ : Useful X : Why did I have to take this? : If the field is left blank, it is neutral * : Grad course Physics (P), computer science is (CS), both is (B) Class Description Worth Subject Discrete Math First exposure to formal proofs and real math.. ✓ CS Diff EQ ✓ P Waves/Optics University Physics 3 essentially tuned for phycisists. ✓ P Engineering lab Just python 101. X CS TX government All I remember is the Railroad commission is in charge of oil, not railroads. X B C++ 1 Basic C++ and OOP. CS Mechanics lab The same lab as in highschool AP Physics C, I had to take it for my CS degree. X CS Linear Alg ✓ B Modern Physics Covers physics from early 1900s, old QM, some SR, particle in a box, UV catastrophe, etc. P Math Methods P Data structures/algorithms C++ but actually this time, with linked lists, stacks, etc. Actually taught programming skills. ✓ CS Programming Languages Java/Scheme (for some god forsaken reason). X CS Computer organization Not too useful but interesting to me. We started with nand gates and ended up building a CPU. We ended the class with a fully functioning virtual computer for which we built the parts 1 by 1, IO, etc. ✓ CS Mechanics ✓✓ P E&M Ergin told me to do a project on Born-Infeld Electrodynamics which was my first exposure to HET concepts through D-branes. ✓✓ P Quantum mechanics * ✓✓ P Computer systems Network stuff, basically how computers talk to each other. ✓ CS Programming studio This was atrocious. Basically how to github and make an app. I learned nothing here. I was the manager for the app we made and coded literally 2 lines in python. The only other thing I had to do was set up meetings and make sure people were on time. For some reason I got glowing reviews from my colleagues and instructor. XXX CS Circuits I learned what an Op amp was and played around with a raspberry pi. ✓ P Statistics This was just AP statistics. We had to pay to access the website to do homework. There was a 10 day free trial and all the assignments were already loaded so I finished the whole course in a week. X CS Analysis of algorithms Actual computer science, P=NP type things. CS Artificial Intelligence Classic AIs starting from the mid 1900s to modern day. This was with my CS advisor and there was a good mix between practice and theory. A lot of first order calculus. ✓ CS Statistical mechanics AKA lunch and boba time (ft Diego and Ian). ✓✓ P Solid state * This class was taught by Valery Pokrovsky and convinced me to go into theory. ✓ P General Relativity * This class was taught by Chris Pope and convinced me to go into HET. ✓✓ P Deep learning * I thought this would be more theory. Instead it was just a class on how to use tensorflow. X CS Numerical methods I don’t even remember what I did in this class besides Newton’s method. For some reason, this is the class that gave me my math minor. B Performance in world cultures X B Experimental physics lab Classic physics experiments. Hydrogen lines, measuring speed of light, etc. ✓ P QFT * To be honest, in my first pass of QFT, I did the calculations, but did not really understand what was going on. ✓✓ P Parallel computing Not too useful, but an interesting topic. I did some interesting projects and the class allowed for clever solutions if you knew some math. ✓ CS CS seminar I listened to 10 random companies tell me why I should work for them. XXX CS Ethics Intro to ethics. Kantianism vs Utilitarianism, etc. ✓ B String theory * I took string theory with Katrin Becker. It was genuinely a great course and I wish I had more mathematical development when I took it. Funnily enough, we followed Polchinski for most of the course before pivoting hard to flux compactifications towards then end. ✓✓ P Graduate
The graduate classes were all useful in some extent. They were either good refreshers or I chose to take them for my own curiosity. Therefore, I’ll just list them.
E&M QM QFT I, II String Theory I, II GR Quantum Info Standard Model/Particle Physics SUSY I tested out of classical mechanics and stat mech.
I may sit in on some graduate math courses. Maybe algebraic geometry or some special topics courses.
